Segmented Fields: String Packing Codes (SC and SU)

Segmented fields provide an alternative storage method for large amounts of data that would be impractical to store in a normal database column. Large data objects can include audio- and video clips, or objects that functionally belong to other applications but are under the control of Uniface. Such objects are referred to as Binary Large Objects (BLOBs).

BLOBs are used to store a pointer, address, or filename in the database that refers to the actual data, but not to store the data itself. The actual data can be stored as a disk file or (heap) memory area. The physical implementation depends on your particular database, but is of no concern to the designer or user. The database connector handles this transparently.

If a DBMS supports a BLOB data type (the actual name of the data type is database-specific), the database connector maps segmented string packing codes to that data type. The maximum size of a segmented string field in Uniface is about 4 GB. However, your particular DBMS may have a smaller maximum size for BLOB data types. For more information, see the connector documentation for your DBMS.

If your DBMS does not support a BLOB data type, the database connector automatically creates a variable-length field to hold the data at the end of the entity. The name of this field is: S_ FieldName, where FieldName is the name of the segmented string field you defined in your entity. The segmented string field only contains a pointer to the variable-length field. Although this is completely transparent to the Uniface designer and user, you can see the effects when you look at your data outside Uniface.

Defining a Length

Be careful when defining a length with segmented string fields. If a length is specified, for example SC100, Uniface only allows 100 characters to be entered in the field in the component and, therefore, stored in the DBMS. This is because Uniface sets the syntax definition to a maximum length of 100. This is the case whatever the capabilities of the underlying DBMS. For more information about the use of segmented strings, see your DBMS documentation.

CAUTION: If optimistic locking is set, segmented fields are not compared when the write ProcScript statement is issued, and no error message is displayed if the stored data has changed since the last read. This can lead to inconsistencies in the stored segmented field data. To avoid this, use optimistic locking in combination with U_VERSION.
Related Links
  • Packing Codes for Other Data

    String, Image, and Raw data can be mapped to a variety of packing codes, including W for Unicode characters.

  • U_VERSION for Improving Locking Performance

    Uniface supports a version mechanism which can improve performance by using a field called U_VERSION. U_VERSION is a one-byte field which you must assign to an entity for it to work. In other words, it is not automatically available.

  • Database Connectors

    To access, retrieve, and store data, Uniface provides database connectors that translate Uniface I/O requests into the target database's data definition and manipulation languages.
